title: Spun caramel and caramel shards
subtitle: Dry caramel taken to the hard-crack stage for decoration
excerpt: Spun caramel (thin threads of amber sugar draped over an oiled dome or spun across rolling pins) and caramel shards are both made from the same base: dry caramel cooked to hard-crack stage, 155°C. The skills are similar; the applications are different. Both need a sugar thermometer and respect for hot syrup.
sourceNotes: Spun sugar and pulled caramel decoration are documented in confectionery and patisserie manuals from the nineteenth century onward. Mrs A.B. Marshall, whose confectionery works of the 1880s, 1890s are widely reproduced in historical baking archives, describes spun sugar and caramel cage work in detail. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes caramel and sugar-work methods in the confectionery section. The hard-crack stage (155°C / 310°F) is the canonical decorating temperature across all English-language sugar-work references.

content.text: Hot caramel burns severely. Keep a bowl of iced water nearby. If caramel contacts skin, cool immediately under running cold water for at least ten minutes. Keep children and pets out of the kitchen during sugar work.

Spun sugar looks fragile and difficult; it is actually quick once the caramel is ready. The challenge is timing: the sugar must be hot enough to thread but cool enough not to run. Practice with shards first. They require less precision.

Combine the {{granulated-sugar}} and {{glucose-syrup}} in a small, heavy saucepan. Do not stir once the heat goes on; swirl the pan gently instead. Cook over medium heat, clipping the sugar thermometer to the side. The
is 155°C. Remove the pan from the heat at 153°C. The residual heat will take it to 155°C. Set the base of the pan briefly in cold water to arrest cooking.

Pour the caramel onto a silicone mat or a lightly oiled baking tray. Leave it flat or tilt the tray to make a thin sheet. Allow to cool completely (about 10 minutes) until brittle. Break into irregular shards. Push them upright into buttercream or whipped cream on the finished cake.
Spun sugar: flick across oiled rolling pins

Allow the caramel to cool in the pan until it threads when you lift a spoon (about 2 to 3 minutes after removing from heat). Hold the pan over two rolling pins laid parallel, about 30 cm apart, with a silicone mat beneath. Using a fork, flick the caramel back and forth in rapid arcs; fine threads will fall across the pins. Gather the threads into a nest and use within the hour.
